question: Where do you see yourself in ten years?
lookingFor: A grounded sense of direction — ambition paired with realistic self-awareness, not a rehearsed job title.

bullets: Give a direction or field, not an over-precise role
Show that the goal is genuinely yours, not just impressive-sounding
Acknowledge that the path will evolve without sounding aimless
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: In ten years I'd like to be working on renewable energy infrastructure — specifically the planning and finance side, where decisions get made about which projects actually get built. I don't know exactly what my title will be, and I'm fine with that, because the problems in that space are changing fast. What I'm clear on is the kind of work I want to do and the impact I want to have, and this course is the foundation that makes it credible.

commonMistakes: Naming a precise job title that sounds recited and generic
A goal that has no connection to what you're applying to study
Being so vague it suggests you have no real direction
followUps: What's the first concrete step you'd take after graduating to move toward that?

question: What do you hope to achieve in your first year at university?
lookingFor: Practical and personal ambitions that show you've thought seriously about what university life actually offers beyond lectures.

bullets: Mention academic goals specifically tied to the course, not just 'do well'
Include at least one goal around skills, people, or experiences outside class
Show you understand that year one sets the foundation
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Academically, I want to build genuinely strong foundations in the core modules rather than coast on what I already know — I've seen people underestimate first-year content and pay for it later. Beyond that, I want to find one or two societies that connect to my interests, because I learn a lot from people who've been doing something longer than I have. And honestly, I want to get comfortable asking for help earlier rather than trying to figure everything out alone first.

commonMistakes: 'Get good grades' as the only answer
Plans so packed they're clearly unrealistic
No mention of academic substance at all — all social
followUps: What would you do if first year turned out to be harder than you expected?

question: What kind of career are you hoping this degree leads to?
lookingFor: A coherent link between the course and a realistic career direction — shows the application is deliberate, not default.

bullets: Name a field or type of work, not just a job title
Connect it back to something genuine — an experience, a value, a curiosity
Acknowledge the degree as a step, not a guaranteed destination
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'm drawn to public health research — specifically work that translates data into policy recommendations that reach people who wouldn't otherwise benefit from it. That interest started when a relative went through a long, confusing diagnosis and I saw how much the system's communication failed her. I'm not locked into one specific role, but I know the kind of contribution I want to make, and this degree puts me where the relevant skills are built.

commonMistakes: Naming an elite job title with no supporting story
A career that has no obvious connection to the subject
Sounding like the career is an afterthought — 'I'll figure it out later'
followUps: Have you spoken to anyone working in that field? What did you learn?

question: Why do you want to go to university rather than starting work or doing an apprenticeship?
lookingFor: A genuine, considered case for the undergraduate route — shows the choice is deliberate, not just the default.

bullets: Name what university specifically offers that the alternatives don't, for you
Tie it to your subject and goals — not just 'the experience'
Show you actually considered the alternatives, not dismissed them
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I thought about it seriously. Apprenticeships made a lot of sense for some of my friends, and I respect that route. But for the work I want to do in materials science, the research depth and the lab time you only get at degree level aren't optional extras — they're the core of what the job actually requires. I also think I'd regret not having three years to go deep on the ideas before I'm working within an employer's constraints. It's not about prestige, it's about what I actually need to do the work well.

commonMistakes: Dismissing apprenticeships without having actually considered them
Falling back on 'university is just what you do after A-levels'
Giving only social or lifestyle reasons with no academic substance
followUps: What would have to be true for you to feel the degree was worth it?

question: Do you have any plans beyond your undergraduate degree — postgraduate study, research, anything like that?
lookingFor: Intellectual curiosity and forward-thinking — an applicant who sees the degree as a launchpad, not a finish line.

bullets: Share a genuine direction without over-committing to an unrealistic plan
Show that the answer connects to something you already care about
Demonstrate you understand that undergraduate study informs those plans
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'm genuinely open to it. If I discover during my degree that I want to go deep on a particular research question, I'd seriously consider a master's or even a PhD — I've had a conversation with my cousin who went that route and it helped me see what that commitment actually looks like. But I want to let the undergraduate work shape that rather than commit now to something I don't yet have the context to evaluate properly. What I do know is that I want to keep learning formally for as long as it's the right thing for the work I want to do.

commonMistakes: A perfectly packaged plan that sounds rehearsed and insincere
'No' with no further thought
Answering only in terms of career advancement rather than intellectual interest
followUps: Is there a particular area within the subject you could imagine researching?

question: What kind of impact do you hope to have on the world through your work?
lookingFor: A genuine sense of purpose that goes beyond personal success — values the applicant has actually thought through.

bullets: Name a specific kind of impact, not a sweeping aspiration to 'change the world'
Ground it in something real — a problem you've seen, a community you care about
Show that the degree connects to this impact
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'm most drawn to work that makes complex things more accessible — whether that's clearer scientific communication, better-designed public services, or tools that help people who aren't experts navigate systems built for experts. I grew up watching family members struggle to understand legal and medical documents, and something about that never left me. I don't expect to single-handedly fix that, but I'd like my work to move in that direction, and studying communication and information design is how I start.

commonMistakes: 'I want to make the world a better place' with no specifics
Impact framed entirely as personal achievement or recognition
No connection between the stated impact and the degree you're applying for
followUps: What's one specific problem you'd most want your career to help solve?

question: What if you complete this degree and your career plans have completely changed — how would you feel about that?
lookingFor: Emotional maturity and adaptability — comfort with uncertainty rather than a rigid plan that ignores reality.

bullets: Accept that change is likely and frame it positively
Show that you value the degree for its transferable value, not just one career path
Reflect on what it means to make a good decision under uncertainty
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think I'd feel okay about it, honestly. I've already changed my mind once — I was convinced I wanted to go into architecture until I realised it was a specific problem in architecture I cared about, not the profession. So I know my thinking evolves. What I'm more confident about than the destination is the way of thinking this subject builds in me — analytical, evidence-driven, curious. Those travel well, whatever direction I end up going.

commonMistakes: Insisting your plans will never change — sounds rigid and unaware
Treating the possibility of change as failure
Having no rationale for the degree beyond the specific career you named
followUps: What part of this subject do you think would be valuable regardless of the exact career you end up in?

question: What skills do you most want to develop during your time at university?
lookingFor: Self-awareness about current gaps and a realistic sense of what the degree can help build.

bullets: Name specific skills, not vague virtues like 'leadership'
Connect at least one skill to the subject itself
Show you understand your own starting point honestly
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I want to get much better at structured argumentation — I can reason well in my head, but putting it into writing that holds up under scrutiny is harder than it looks and I know it's something I need to work at. Beyond that, I want to develop confidence presenting to audiences who don't already agree with me, which is what seminars and debates can push you toward. And I'd like to get more comfortable with quantitative methods, because I've been more of a qualitative thinker and I don't want that to be a ceiling.

commonMistakes: Skills so generic they mean nothing — 'communication', 'teamwork' with no context
Presenting yourself as already fully developed with nothing to learn
Skills entirely unrelated to the course or academic life
followUps: Which of those would you say is your weakest starting point right now?

subtopic: Role of the degree in your plan
question: Why is a degree the right vehicle for what you want to do — couldn't you get there another way?
lookingFor: A thoughtful defence of the undergraduate route grounded in the specific nature of the subject and the applicant's goals.

bullets: Name what this degree specifically provides that self-study or work couldn't replicate
Tie the answer to your goals, not just a generic case for higher education
Acknowledge the alternatives honestly before dismissing them
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: For some goals, probably yes — there are great self-taught designers, writers, coders. But for what I want to do in clinical psychology, the degree isn't a nice-to-have, it's a literal licensing requirement, and even beyond that, the supervised clinical placements and the depth of theory aren't something you can replicate on YouTube. I've also found that I learn better in dialogue — tutorial discussion has pushed my thinking further than working alone. So it's the right vehicle for the content and for how I actually learn.

commonMistakes: Struggling to answer because you never really considered the question
Only citing institutional prestige or credentials, not the learning itself
Dismissing alternatives without engaging with them
followUps: What would you do if you discovered you could achieve your goal without the degree?

question: What kind of working environment do you think you'll thrive in after you graduate?
lookingFor: Self-awareness about working style and environment — shows the applicant has genuinely thought about fit beyond just a job title.

bullets: Be specific about environment — team size, pace, type of problem, autonomy vs structure
Ground it in evidence from your own experience, not guessing
Show you know yourself well enough to be honest about what doesn't work for you
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: From doing group projects and some volunteering, I've learned I do my best thinking when I have autonomy on how I approach a problem, but I want people around me to pressure-test the work. So probably a mid-sized team with a strong culture of feedback, rather than total isolation or a huge corporation where work can disappear into a process. I also work better when the purpose is visible — I found it much harder to stay motivated on abstract tasks than on ones where the outcome was tangible.

commonMistakes: A vague ideal with no grounding in actual experience
Describing a perfect environment that sounds designed to impress
Contradicting earlier statements about goals or working style
followUps: Have you had any experience yet that's tested what kind of environment suits you?

question: Who or what has had the biggest influence on the direction you want to take?
lookingFor: Authentic motivation with a real origin story — not a famous name dropped without substance.

bullets: Name a specific person, moment, or experience — not a vague 'passion'
Show why it influenced you, not just that it did
Connect it to the direction you've taken or the course you're applying for
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: It was a teacher I had at fourteen who ran a lunchtime philosophy club. She had a way of making you realise that questions you'd always assumed were settled actually weren't — that everything was up for examination if you had the tools. I started reading outside class for the first time, just because I wanted to keep that feeling going. She didn't push me toward any particular career, but she fundamentally changed what kind of thinker I want to be, and that's what led me toward law, where argument and evidence are the whole game.

commonMistakes: Naming a famous person you've never actually engaged with in depth
A story with no clear link to the direction you're actually pursuing
Being so vague that the influence could apply to any subject
followUps: Has that influence held up as you've learned more, or have you revised your thinking?

question: Do you ever think about starting something of your own — a project, a business, a social enterprise?
lookingFor: Entrepreneurial curiosity or a well-reasoned preference for another path — either is fine; the thinking matters more than the answer.

bullets: Be honest — yes or no is fine, but explain the reasoning
If yes, say what kind of thing and why — not just 'my own company'
Show that you've connected this ambition (or lack of it) to your subject
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I do, actually. I keep coming back to the idea of a platform that helps first-generation students navigate university applications — something that gives them what their peers with connected parents take for granted. I'm nowhere near ready to build it, but it's something I think about a lot. Studying sociology and data will give me a much better understanding of both the structural problem and what a solution would need to address to actually work. Whether I do it or contribute to someone else doing it better, I want to be useful to that problem.

commonMistakes: A grandiose startup idea with no real grounding in the subject
Dismissing the idea entirely as though ambition is suspect
An idea with no connection to the course you're applying for
followUps: What would have to be true for you to feel ready to pursue that?

question: What do you hope to contribute to your field over the course of your career?
lookingFor: Ambition that's grounded in genuine understanding of the field — shows the applicant has thought beyond getting a job.

bullets: Name a specific gap, problem, or frontier in the field you want to address
Show you understand what's already been done and where the gaps are
Frame ambition as contribution, not personal achievement
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'm most interested in the gap between what we know from behavioural research about decision-making and what actually gets applied in public health messaging. There's a lot of well-funded science that doesn't translate because the communication step is an afterthought. I'd like to work on closing that — making research land with the people it's meant to reach. That's a career-long project more than a single milestone, but it's the thread I want to pull on.

commonMistakes: An answer entirely framed in terms of personal success or recognition
A contribution so vague it could apply to any field
Overclaiming — promising to solve problems that entire fields have worked on for decades
followUps: Is there any work already being done in that area that you admire or want to build on?

question: What does success look like to you — how will you know your career is going well?
lookingFor: A personal and considered definition of success that goes beyond conventional markers like salary or title.

bullets: Define success in terms that feel genuinely yours, not borrowed from a generic template
Include both professional and personal dimensions
Show some awareness that the definition may evolve
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think success for me means doing work that I'd find meaningful even when it's hard, and being able to look back at the decisions I made without cringing. Professionally, that probably means working on problems I think actually matter, with people I respect. I'm aware that money matters and I'm not pretending otherwise, but I'd find it hard to stay motivated purely for it. I also want to stay curious — if I'm still learning in twenty years, that's a good sign to me that I got the direction roughly right.

commonMistakes: Success defined only as salary, status, or prestigious employer
A definition so idealistic it ignores real constraints
Repeating back what you think the interviewer wants to hear
followUps: Are there any models — people or organisations — whose version of success you admire?

question: Do you see yourself working internationally, or is your focus more local or national?
lookingFor: Genuine reflection on the scope of the applicant's ambitions — and awareness of what global or local work actually entails.

bullets: Give an honest answer — either direction is fine with reasoning
Show you understand what international or local work actually looks like in this field
Connect the answer to your values or goals, not just aspiration
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'm drawn to international work, but for specific reasons rather than just novelty. The policy problems I care about — climate adaptation, water access — don't stop at borders, and the most interesting approaches are being developed in very different contexts across the world. I'd want to work in places where those problems are most acute, and bring that learning back into conversation elsewhere. But I'd want to do it in a way that's genuinely collaborative rather than parachuting in with answers.

commonMistakes: International ambition framed purely as adventure or prestige
No awareness of the practical or ethical complexity of working across cultures
A local focus with no reasoning — sounds like a lack of ambition rather than a genuine choice
followUps: Have you had any experience that's given you a realistic sense of what that would involve?

question: Did you consider taking a gap year? What would you have done with one?
lookingFor: Thoughtfulness about time and opportunity — whether or not they took a gap year, the applicant should have considered it deliberately.

bullets: Show the decision was considered, not a default
If no, say what made university now feel right
If yes, explain what you did and what it gave you that's relevant
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I did think about it. I looked at structured volunteer placements abroad and a research internship. In the end I chose to come straight to university because I didn't want to lose the academic momentum — I was in the middle of a really engaging extended essay and I wanted to keep going with that energy. But I don't see it as foreclosing anything; I'd seriously consider spending a summer or even a year later in my studies doing something applied and different. I want to do that deliberately, not just as a default break.

commonMistakes: 'No, I just wanted to come to university' with no reflection
A gap year answer that's all about travel with no intellectual or personal growth
Critiquing other people's choices about gap years
followUps: If you had unlimited time and money, what would your ideal gap year have looked like?

subtopic: Connecting past experience to future goals
question: How have your experiences outside the classroom shaped what you want to do with your future?
lookingFor: A through-line connecting real-world experience to future direction — shows the goals aren't abstract but lived.

bullets: Name specific experiences — volunteering, work, projects, personal events
Show the link between what you experienced and the direction you now want to pursue
Avoid summarising everything; pick one or two that had real weight
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I spent two summers helping run a community literacy programme for adults, and it changed what I want to do more than any lesson did. I saw firsthand how much of what holds people back isn't ability — it's access and confidence. I came away wanting to work on structural solutions rather than one-off interventions, which is why I'm drawn to policy and systems design rather than direct service roles. That experience made my goals feel urgent in a way they weren't before.

commonMistakes: Listing experiences without connecting them to future direction
Picking only academic experiences when the question asks about outside the classroom
A connection so vague it could work for any course
followUps: What's the one insight from those experiences you'd most want to carry into your career?

question: What do you see yourself doing five years after graduating?
lookingFor: A realistic near-term picture that bridges the degree and a longer ambition — not just a repeat of the ten-year answer.

bullets: Be specific about the type of work, not just a sector
Show awareness that five years post-graduation is still early career — calibrate accordingly
Connect it to skills you'll have built through the degree
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Five years out, I'd realistically expect to be two or three years into a role — probably in a mid-level position in UX research or product strategy, having done an entry-level stint first and built up enough experience to take on more complex briefs. I'd like to have worked on at least one project I can point to as genuinely mine, not just a contribution. And I'd want to have some clarity by then about whether I want to go deeper as a specialist or move toward a broader role — five years is about enough time to know that.

commonMistakes: Describing a position that's unrealistically senior for five years post-graduation
Giving the exact same answer as the ten-year question
No mention of learning or growth — just a destination
followUps: What's one thing you could do in your degree that would make that five-year vision more achievable?

question: What would you do if your first-choice career path turned out not to be available to you after graduating?
lookingFor: Resilience and transferable thinking — an applicant who can adapt without having no plan at all.

bullets: Show you've thought about adjacent paths, not just one narrow corridor
Frame adaptability as a strength, not a defeat
Make it clear you understand what skills from the degree are genuinely transferable
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My first instinct would be to look at what's adjacent rather than completely different. If I couldn't get into the specific research role I'm aiming for, the skills I'll have — data analysis, writing, a systems perspective — transfer into policy analysis, consulting, or sector research roles that sit close to the same problems. I'm not treating those as consolation prizes; they're legitimately interesting paths I'd pursue with real commitment. I think the mistake would be defining success so narrowly that any deviation feels like failure.

commonMistakes: 'That won't happen' — refusing to engage with the question
A backup plan that has nothing to do with the degree or skills developed
Sounding so flexible it suggests no real motivation for the first-choice path
followUps: Have you looked at any of those adjacent roles in any detail?

subtopic: Making a difference vs. financial security
question: How do you think about the tension between doing meaningful work and earning a good living — do you see that as a real trade-off?
lookingFor: Honest, grounded thinking about values — not a naive 'money doesn't matter' or a cynical 'only the money matters.'

bullets: Acknowledge the tension is real without catastrophising it
Share your own position on it honestly, not what you think they want to hear
Show some awareness of how your chosen field sits in that landscape
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think it's a real tension but not always as stark as it's presented. The field I'm going into — environmental consultancy — does pay reasonably, so I'm not facing a dramatic choice between impact and a roof over my head. I'm aware that could look different if I wanted to work in the third sector or in academia. I try not to be idealistic about it: financial security matters, both for me and for the people I'll eventually be responsible for. But I know that I struggle to stay motivated without a sense of purpose, so it's not purely calculus. I want both, and I'm trying to build a path where both are possible.

commonMistakes: Pretending money doesn't matter at all — sounds dishonest
Pretending only money matters — sounds like you don't care about the subject
No awareness of how this tension actually plays out in your specific field
followUps: At what point would the financial trade-off start to feel too steep for you?

question: Do you see yourself in a leadership role eventually, or are you more drawn to specialist or individual contributor work?
lookingFor: Honest self-knowledge about working style and ambition — either answer is valid with good reasoning.

bullets: Give a genuine answer — both leadership and specialist paths are respectable
Ground it in self-knowledge, not what sounds impressive
Show you understand what leadership actually involves beyond a title
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Honestly, I'm more drawn to depth than breadth at this stage. I find that I do my best work when I'm fully in a problem rather than managing across many — and I've seen a lot of people promoted into leadership because they were great at the technical work, then end up doing neither well. That said, I don't want to shut the door on it entirely. I think leadership that's genuinely in service of a team's work, rather than a status pursuit, is something I could find meaningful. I want to get good enough first to have something worth sharing.

commonMistakes: Defaulting to 'yes, I want to be a leader' because it sounds ambitious
A specialist preference that sounds like a fear of responsibility rather than a genuine choice
No reasoning — just a preference with nothing behind it
followUps: Have you had any experience leading others, even informally? What did you take from it?

question: How do you plan to keep learning and staying current in your field once you've graduated?
lookingFor: A habit of intellectual curiosity that's independent of formal education — not just 'I'll do a master's.'

bullets: Mention specific ways you already stay current in your area of interest
Show that learning is a habit, not just a response to formal deadlines
Demonstrate awareness that your field evolves and that you'll need to evolve with it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I already follow a handful of journals and researchers in this space and read more than I'm required to, so I think that habit will carry over. Beyond reading, I think communities matter — being around people who are working on new things is how you stay sharp. Professionally, I'd want to look for organisations that invest in development and treat conferences or continuing education as normal, not a perk. And I find that teaching or explaining things is one of the best ways to find out what you don't actually understand — I'd want to find ways to do that too.

commonMistakes: Treating the degree as the end of learning rather than the start
'I'll do another qualification' with nothing more specific
No evidence of current habits that would suggest the plan is realistic
followUps: Is there anything in your field right now that you're actively trying to understand better?

subtopic: Sector preference: public, private, or third
question: Do you see yourself working in the public sector, private sector, or a non-profit context — and why?
lookingFor: A considered view of where the applicant's goals, values, and skills best fit — not a reflexive or ideological answer.

bullets: Give a real answer, not a diplomatic hedge across all three
Ground the preference in values or what the work actually involves, not just image
Show awareness of what each sector actually looks and feels like
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My instinct is toward the public or third sector, at least initially. The problems I most want to work on — housing, mental health services — live in that space, and I'd find it hard to be fully engaged on them from inside a private consultancy where the client relationship shapes what questions you can ask. That said, I don't think private firms are off the table — some of the most interesting applied research I've read came out of consultancies embedded in those sectors. I want the work to be close to the problem, and I'll follow that wherever it leads.

commonMistakes: 'I'm open to anything' — sounds like no genuine thinking about it
Ideological dismissal of an entire sector with no nuance
A preference with no grounding in knowledge of what those sectors actually look like
followUps: Have you had any exposure to the sector you're drawn to — even through research or conversation?

subtopic: Motivation when plans don't go to plan
question: What keeps you motivated when a goal you've worked toward doesn't work out?
lookingFor: Emotional resilience and self-regulation — the ability to recover direction after disappointment without external scaffolding.

bullets: Give a genuine, personal answer — not a motivational poster
Show that you've actually experienced setback, not hypothesised about it
Demonstrate you can reorient without catastrophising
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I had a competition I'd worked toward for months go badly, and what pulled me out wasn't inspiration — it was just getting back to the smaller daily work. I find that when the big goal falls away, the habits are still there, and that's usually enough to keep moving until I've had time to regroup. I also try to separate the goal from the work. The goal was to win; the work was to build a skill. The skill is still there even when the goal isn't. That reframe helps me more than any pep talk.

commonMistakes: 'I never give up' — sounds rehearsed and avoids the real question
An answer that requires external validation or support to recover — shows fragility
No actual example — speaking only in hypotheticals when asked what keeps you going
followUps: Can you think of a specific moment where you had to do that?

question: What do you hope people say about your work, or the difference you made, at the end of your career?
lookingFor: A sense of purpose and values that goes beyond conventional achievement — how the applicant defines a life well worked.

bullets: Speak to quality and impact, not status or recognition
Ground the aspiration in the work you've said you want to do
Be honest — this is a values question more than a career question
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'd want people to say the work was honest and that it made something genuinely easier for people who needed it. I'm not particularly driven by recognition — I find I do better work when I'm not thinking about how it'll look. But I care a lot about whether it was actually useful. If someone who wasn't going to get a fair shot at something ended up getting one because of something I contributed to — in policy, in design, in research — that would feel like enough. I think that's the ambition that sits behind all the more specific goals.

commonMistakes: Framing legacy entirely as reputation or fame
An answer so abstract it reveals nothing about the applicant's actual values
A mismatch between the stated legacy and the career goals described elsewhere in the interview
followUps: Is there anyone whose career legacy you look at and think 'that's what I'm aiming for'?
